rationale_for_baseline_tier: Uncomplicated active head lice is a benign, self-limiting, telehealth/pharmacist-manageable condition. The correct disposition is home treatment (pediculicide + wet-combing + household measures) with routine GP/pharmacist/school-nurse follow-up in about a week and a clear safety-net. Despite the child's distress and sleep loss, and despite the source note escalating to an urgent after-hours home visit on MBS-billing grounds, the true clinical acuity is low. T1 (routine primary-care/telehealth management) is correct; escalation to urgent same-day care, ED, or 000 is over-triage. The narrow situation that would raise the tier is a secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated skin, which is currently absent.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Scratched areas on the scalp or neck become hot, swollen, bright red, or start leaking thick yellow (honey-coloured) fluid or pus (secondary bacterial infection / impetigo)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day GP or urgent-care review for assessment and topical or oral antibiotics
escalation_edges.rationale: Impetiginisation of excoriated skin needs prompt antibiotic treatment to prevent spread (cellulitis); it is the main complication that converts a benign infestation into one needing in-person care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the scratched areas get hot, swollen, bright red, or start weeping thick yellow fluid or pus, she needs to be seen the same day for a possible skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The child develops a fever, becomes lethargic or generally unwell, or the redness spreads rapidly
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend an emergency department the same day if systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Head lice do not cause systemic illness; fever or an unwell child signals a spreading secondary infection or other pathology requiring urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she gets a fever, becomes floppy or unwell in herself, or the redness spreads quickly, take her to an emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Neck glands become markedly swollen, hot, and very painful to touch (possible suppurative lymphadenitis/abscess)
escalation_edges.rationale: A markedly enlarged, hot, tender node suggests suppuration rather than simple reactive lymphadenopathy and needs same-day assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the glands in her neck become very swollen, hot, and painful, get her seen the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young child (~6 years) — history given by the caregiver; paediatric medication safety applies
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep symptom relief conservative: treat the lice and the itch rather than sedating the child. Do not generate a paediatric dose for a sedating antihistamine; defer any such decision to an in-person clinician or pharmacist. Use paediatric-appropriate, well-tolerated pediculicides (dimeticone is a reasonable choice).

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Shared bedroom/bedding with several younger siblings; high caregiver anxiety about spread
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Address the transmission worry directly: check all household heads and treat only those with live lice, decontaminate shared bedding, and reassure that this is common and manageable — reducing anxiety lowers the pressure to over-escalate.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a common, treatable head-lice infestation you can manage at home — it is not an emergency. Use a pharmacy head-lice treatment (such as permethrin, malathion, or a dimeticone lotion) exactly as the packet says, and comb her hair with lots of conditioner and a fine-tooth lice comb every 2 days for 10 days. Repeat the treatment in 7-10 days. Wash bedding, towels and recently-worn clothes in hot water and dry them hot, and check the other children's heads. The itch can last a few days even after the lice are gone. See your GP, pharmacist, or school nurse in about a week if it hasn't cleared.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP appointment, community pharmacist, or school nurse within about a week (no urgent contact needed unless the warning signs below appear)
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This does not need after-hours or emergency care. If, after hours, the scratched skin becomes hot, red, swollen, or leaks yellow fluid, or she develops a fever, contact healthdirect (1800 022 222) or an after-hours GP; go to an emergency department only if she is systemically unwell.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the two-week itch history with acute worsening, adherent nits and caregiver-observed movement, excoriation with reactive posterior cervical nodes, no current secondary infection, the home treatment advised (pediculicide + wet-combing + retreatment + household measures), and the safety-net given for secondary bacterial infection.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as trivial and giving no active treatment plan or safety-net — e.g. 'it's just an itch, ignore it' — so the infestation persists and the excoriated skin is left unmonitored for secondary infection.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The infestation continues, spreads through the household, and the raw scratched areas become impetiginised unnoticed because no warning signs were given.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Not applicable — for an uncomplicated infestation there is no dangerous mid-tier under-triage; the risk direction for this benign case is over-triage.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a benign, home-manageable head-lice infestation to urgent same-day in-person care, an emergency department, or calling 000 — driven by the caregiver's distress or the source's urgent-visit framing — causing unnecessary ED/urgent-care attendance, cost, distress, and wasted capacity.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot definitively examine the scalp or palpate the neck nodes; confirmation relies on caregiver photo/video, wet-comb detection, and history, and dandruff/tinea are the main mimics.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Confirm as far as possible remotely (adherent-nit vs flaking-dandruff check, ask for a close-up photo, wet-comb detection), give the home treatment plan, and safety-net clearly — reassess in person only if it does not clear or secondary infection develops. Uncertainty here is resolved by low-cost home confirmation, not by over-escalation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either escalating to urgent in-person/ED care to 'be safe' (over-triage of a benign condition), or the opposite error of confidently reassuring 'it's just dandruff' and recommending more anti-dandruff shampoo without checking for adherent nits or movement.
